one_line_takeaway: SARE applies Sharpness-Aware Minimization to MLLM hallucination unlearning, framing erasure as a targeted min-max optimization that flattens the loss landscape so hallucination suppression survives relearning, LoRA fine-tuning, and adversarial prompting far better than the EFUF baseline, without materially degrading generation quality or general reasoning benchmarks.
key_finding: On LLaVA-v1.5-7B, SARE reduces CHAIRs from the vanilla baseline of 49.4 to 34.4 (Origin, before any perturbation) versus EFUF's 41.8, and POPE improves to 86.7 versus EFUF's 85.9 and the vanilla baseline's 85.3. Under Relearn 140, SARE's Humans stays at 21.0 versus EFUF's Humans rising to 29.0 (near the pre-unlearning level); under LoRA fine-tuning, SARE holds CHAIRi at 17.4 versus EFUF's 20.8. On mPLUG-Owl-7B, SARE cuts CHAIRs from the vanilla baseline 69.6 to 37.3 versus EFUF's 43.6, and under Adversarial Prompting SARE keeps CHAIRs at 37.5 while EFUF spikes to 50.0 (close to baseline). Generation quality is largely preserved: SARE's Bleu-4 on LLaVA is 18.9 vs EFUF's 18.2, and PPL is 0.101 vs EFUF's 0.113. On general-capability benchmarks (Figure 3), SARE attains the top MME score of 1506 while remaining competitive on GQA, ScienceQA, and QBench against GA, DPO, NPO, and EFUF. Ablations (Table 2, LLaVA) show fine-grained unlearning alone gives only a marginal CHAIRs drop (49.4 to 46.1) with degraded fluency, and sentence-loss-only unlearning achieves an artificially low PPL (0.087) but collapses POPE from 85.3 to 70.0, indicating the model is decoupling from visual input rather than genuinely reducing hallucination -- both ablations motivate the full SARE combination. Efficiency: SARE shows only a 1.07x per-step slowdown versus EFUF on LLaVA-v1.5-7B, attributed to reusing cached visual embeddings for the perturbed forward pass and restricting gradient computation to a lightweight component, while being substantially faster than DPO/NPO.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SARE (Sharpness-Aware Robust Erasure), a machine-unlearning framework for suppressing object hallucinations in multimodal LLMs (MLLMs). The authors first empirically show that existing hallucination-unlearning methods (specifically EFUF) achieve only 'superficial suppression': the unlearned model sits in a sharp minimum of the hallucination loss landscape, so lightweight relearning (fine-tuning on a small amount of the original hallucination-inducing data), standard LoRA fine-tuning, or adversarial prompting rapidly reverses the suppression. To fix this, SARE reformulates hallucination unlearning as a targeted min-max optimization: an inner step finds the worst-case parameter perturbation (approximated via a first-order Taylor expansion of the gradient of the negative/hallucination loss, following SAM) that maximizes hallucination-relapse likelihood, and an outer step minimizes the joint objective (suppress hallucinations on Dneg, preserve grounding on Dpos, preserve fluency on Dsent) under that worst-case perturbation. This explicitly flattens the loss landscape around the unlearned state so the erasure is robust to subsequent weight shifts, while reusing EFUF's existing automated data-curation pipeline (CLIP-score-based thresholding into Dpos/Dneg/Dsent subsets).
threat_model: Not a security threat model in the adversary/attacker-vs-defender sense used in AI-security literature. The paper's 'adversarial' settings are robustness stress tests on the unlearning procedure itself: (1) a 'relearning attack' where the already-unlearned model is fine-tuned on a small subset of the original hallucination-inducing data to see if suppressed hallucinations resurface; (2) standard LoRA fine-tuning on ~10,000 samples from the original training set, testing whether ordinary downstream fine-tuning inadvertently reactivates hallucinations; (3) 'adversarial prompting', where the model is given prompts demanding exhaustive object listing to probe instruction-induced hallucination. None of these involve an external attacker targeting confidentiality, integrity, or availability of a deployed system, jailbreaking, prompt injection, or data exfiltration; they are internal robustness/regression tests on a model-editing (unlearning) technique against ordinary continued training. The paper frames this as generalization/geometric-stability research (drawing on SAM), not as a security defense against a malicious party.
limitations: The authors state three limitations explicitly: (1) the unlearning dataset relies on a static CLIP-score-based filtering pipeline that only targets already-known hallucination patterns, so coverage of latent/dormant hallucination triggers that activate on novel or out-of-distribution prompts remains limited, motivating future dynamic data curation; (2) the robust erasure mechanism is currently optimized only for object-existence hallucinations and has not been extended to more nuanced types such as incorrect attributes or fallacious spatial relations, with existing vision-language metrics themselves lacking the fine-grained semantics needed to construct such data; (3) extending the geometric-flattening approach to higher-order abstract-reasoning hallucinations (errors from fallacious logical chains rather than object misidentification) remains an open future challenge. Additionally, exact sharpness metrics were not computed directly for the 7B-parameter models because doing so is computationally prohibitive; the authors instead used degradation under Relearning/LoRA attacks as an empirical proxy for landscape sharpness (an internal methodological caveat, footnoted in Section 4.2).

datasets_used: MSCOCO (3,200 sampled images: 1,600 validation / 1,600 test; ~30,000 unlearning training triplets)
EFUF-derived curated subsets Dpos/Dneg/Dsent via CLIP alignment scores
quantitative_results: LLaVA-v1.5-7B under Relearn 140: EFUF Humans 29.0 vs SARE Humans 21.0
LLaVA-v1.5-7B under LoRA FT: EFUF CHAIRi 20.8 vs SARE CHAIRi 17.4
mPLUG-Owl-7B under Adversarial Prompting: EFUF CHAIRs 50.0 vs SARE CHAIRs 37.5
LLaVA-v1.5-7B Bleu-4: EFUF 18.2 vs SARE 18.9; PPL: EFUF 0.113 vs SARE 0.101
mPLUG-Owl-7B Informativeness: SARE 89.6 vs EFUF 90.0 (0.4 lower)
Ablation on LLaVA (Table 2): Origin CHAIRs 49.4 -> +fine-grained-unlearning-only 46.1 -> +Sentence-Loss-only 37.8 (but POPE collapses 85.3->70.0) -> full SARE 34.4 (POPE 86.7)
MME benchmark: SARE achieves top score of 1506 among Origin/GA/DPO/NPO/EFUF/SARE
Efficiency: SARE incurs only 1.07x per-step slowdown vs EFUF on LLaVA-v1.5-7B; substantially faster than DPO and NPO in A800 GPU hours
